OK, so I want to recap what we did last time. I know at the very end we were going a little fast, so I want to make sure we get back and cover what we talked about at the very end and see how what we did we're doing is actually a form of exploratory data analysis.

So we talked about group by last time and the way it works is you say your data frame group by and then you give it a column that you want to group on, right? And what this does is it says it makes 1 little like mini data frame, 1 sub frame for every unique value in that column.
And then you give it a bunch of columns that you want to do some computation on within each group.

Right. Maybe it's just one, maybe it's multiple, but you give it some columns and then you give it an aggregation function, which is basically telling it what do you want to do for each of those columns within each group, right? And that calculation is done within each group separately.
So as an example, if they have a data frame of people's grades at the end of the semester and.

I run student grades group I section and then say bracket, bracket, midterm one final dot AG mean. So what does this do? It says, OK, we're going to have one group for every section of students. And then I'm only going to look at the midterm and final columns. And within those columns I'm going to call dot AG mean.

So basically this is just computing the average midterm score and final score within each section. So the result looks something like this. For section 101, the average midterm one score was 48%, average final is 55 and so on.
So this is grouped by. Very useful when trying to answer questions about data.

So we ended last time talking about this idea of wanting to find the best election for each party, right? For every party, what election was like the best that they ever did, where best is defined by the highest percentage of votes?

There are other ways we could have defined best, right? Maybe best means most Electoral College votes. But here we happen to define it this way. So this is the way we're going to answer the question. Keep in mind that a lot of times when you're asking questions about your data set, there are multiple ways to define these things, and defining them different ways will give you potentially different answers.

So let's suppose we run elections group by party dot Max, and we saw that this did not work right. It told us that Woodrow Wilson won in 2024 with 61% of the vote, which we know is not true. Why does this fail? Because it finds the Max separately for every single column.

So what we did instead was we took advantage of the fact that group by within each of those little mini data frames, within each of those little subframes, it maintains the order from the original data frame, right? So if your original data frame has like a particular column in alphabetical order, then each of the subframes is going to have that column in alphabetical order. If your data frame is sorted by something from high to low, then each of the subframes are also going to be sorted by that variable title.
So we start by sorting by percent.
So the elections sort value is percent and descending order and that gives us the data frame that looks like this right? Where basically we have every row from highest percent to lowest percent.
And then we take that and we group by party and then take the first item.
What does that mean? Basically, we're saying we take this data frame group by party. So for every party we have one group.

And for every party, we have one group, and within each of those groups, we're going to take the first value. What does it mean to be taking the first value? Well, it just means that we're looking at the top value. What is the top value? It's the one with the highest percent.

So as a quick example, right, this is our original data frame. You know, it has all these different columns in order by year when we sort values and you know we get from highest percent down to lowest percent.

And then when we group by party, the order is preserved. So the Democrat little Democratic Party is little like subframe has high to low, the Republican subframe has high to low, the Libertarian Party has high to low, and so on. And then we just need to pull out the first item on each one.
How do we get the first row in a data frame? We use dot head.

So we say group by party dot head of one. This is a different kind of grouping than like Max or min or sum or mean, but it's really just doing something in a group. It's taking the first item in the group.
And our sort over here guarantees that that first item is going to be the one with the highest percent within the group.
OK, so.

Up until now, we've spent a lot of time on the nuts and bolts of pandas, right? Understanding all the different pandas functions, how they work, what we can do with them. If it's a half, then it's one. If it's, you know, all, all boys or all girls, then it's 0.

It's not the only function we could have used, right? We could use a function like this. We can use a function like this. There's a lot of different possibilities. So it's important to keep in mind that, again, this is a choice that we made. That choice might affect the results.

All right, so that is one example of how we might do some exploratory data analysis, how we might answer some interesting and meaningful questions about a data set using the tools that we have developed.
So all right, now that we have the tools, let's say we get a new data set. What do we need to do, right? What are the questions that we need to answer?
The process of taking a new data set and kind of looking at it and kind of like poking at it and figuring out what's going on is called exploratory data analysis, or EDA.

If you look at, you know, visualization of like, what do data scientists do based on surveys, a huge number of them say they spend a lot of their time doing exploratory data analysis, right? It's a very, very common operation. It's what you'll often spend the most time doing is poking around playing with your data.
But really, I think it's not quite right. Going out, getting more data, realizing you're confused about some columns, going and answering that confusion, and so on.

So when you get a new data set, what do you need to do? So these are four kind of properties of the data set that it's really important to understand and like have an understanding in your mind before you start really like doing all kinds of complex analysis, the kinds of tools that we'll be giving you a little later in the class.
To start, let's think about what is the structure of the data. Well, the structure is the shape of a data file.

So we often work with what's called rectangular data, right? Rectangular data is nice and clean because, you know, we have like clean rows and columns, and we have for every row we have. For every column we have a data point. Or maybe it's missing.
There's two types of rectangular data. There's tables and matrices. So far we've been working with tables. For most of this class we're going to work with tables.

There are also matrices, right? If you have taken or are taking linear algebra, you know you'll be learning all about matrices or have learned all about them and matrix is A2 dimensional. You know, data data just like a table.

But a matrix, all of the data points are all numbers, and they're all the same type, right? In a table, every column can be have a different type and so on. And matrices are manipulated with linear algebra, You know, we do matrix multiplication. So there's a little less flexible. They're more powerful for what they're good at, but they're less flexible, less general. Unlike tables, we're going to spend most of their time in this class of tables in Python or R they're data frames.
So when we have these columns, they're variables of different types. We've kind of seen some of those types already, right? Integers, floats, strings, and so on. So.
Let's talk about what different kinds of variables we might get in a data set.

Broadly, there's two categories of variables. There are qualitative variables, which are things like that are categorical and they're quantitative. So quantitative variables is basically anything numeric, right? Anything you can quantify, add, subtract, look at differences between things like that, then it's numeric, right? If you can do that, then it's quantitative. Otherwise it's qualitative.
So quantitative again is like measurable numeric things like prices, right. Prices you can add. Look at the difference between you can average temperature, age, same thing.
And.

Qualitative can fall into one of two categories. Qualitative variables can be either ordered, in which case they're ordinal, or they can be unordered, in which case they're nominal. So ordered categories, for example, is what grade level is a student in.

Right. If I have a bunch of second graders and a bunch of 4th graders, you know, second graders and 3rd graders, I'm not going to compute the average grade level of my student, right? It doesn't make sense to say the average grade level of these students is 2.3.
No fishing is like 2 point third grade.

But I have a sense that third grade is higher than second grade. So this ordering introduces if there's an ordering, then we call them ordinal, otherwise we call it nominal. This is these are things that are unordered. For example, what brand is your phone right? Is it Apple? Is it Samsung, Nokia, Google, so on Or ID numbers can be nominal as well.
So let's quickly do a couple of examples. CO2 level, right? If I look at CO2 level, what kind of variable is this?
Someone just shout it out.
Quantitative.
Income bracket low, medium or high.
So I heard a mumble, but yes or it sounded like ordinal to me, right? This is qualitative ordinal. Why? Because we have an ordering from low, medium, high.
Race or ethnicity?
A qualitative nominal right because there's no ordering across them political party.

Also nominal. But I could also define political parties on like a spectrum from left to right. Right? I could say this party is further to the left, this party is further to the right. Representation.

So fun fact, zip codes are not actually randomly assigned. There is a structure, a rhyme or reason to them. You know, zip code starting with 9 tend to be, you know, West Coast, Alaska, Hawaii. Here are different values of zip code.
Hide this guy. Umm.

So ZIP codes aren't randomly assigned. And if we think about it, well, we can't really do like math on ZIP codes, right? It doesn't make sense to do like what's the average of 94725 and ZIP code 93102, right? Because the numbers don't make sense as numbers, right? They're just sort of labels. And so because of that, they are really more.
Qualitative and not quantitative.
If we were to store them as integers, well, what could go wrong?
Well, what if we? You know, we say it's categorical, but we like use an integer to store it anyway.

What could, what might happen? Well, if we look up here, think about the Northeast, looking at the hint, we see a bunch of these that start with 0. If we store them as integers, a zip code like you know, 02147 is going to be stored as just 2147. We're going to drop the leading zero. We're not going to see it as a 5 digit number.
So a lot of times when you have qualitative variables, it's generally best practice to store them as strings, even if they happen to look like numbers.
ID numbers maybe a notable exception, but generally you want to store qualitative variables as strings.

OK, so we talked a little bit about structure. We'll come back to structure and different kinds of data a little more, but I want to talk about something important, which is granularity, which is to say how fine or coarse is a particular piece of data within your data set. Quick aside, you know, the word data is technically a plural word. The singular of data is.
Datum or datum. So if you use the word data.

Technically, you really should like pluralize it, but in practice people usually don't care, right? You will hear people say data is, data are, so you will hear me say data is and both data is and data.
Yeah, it's technically incorrect, but in practice it's really fine.
So granularity is basically referring to how fine or coarse is each particular data. What does that mean?

Basically, what that means is for every row, what is every row represent, right? We might have a data set where every row is an individual person, right? And for that person, we have a bunch of information about them. Or we may have a that's sort of fine.
On the other end of the spectrum, we may have a data set that's more coarse, where every row is a group of people.
You know, maybe every row is 1 state, maybe every row is 1 country, right? That has a bunch of different people and we have aggregate information about that group.
So if we have data about sort of single person, single purchase, then that's more fine grained.

Some data sets happen to include summaries or roll-ups. So for example, you may have like a row for every person and then a summary row included. So if you're calculating average, you need to make sure to remove that row from your analysis first.
And when you get data that is coarse, it's important to keep in mind how were the records aggregated right if you have data a data representing an entire state.

How did you compute the average value for every for every state? Did you see someone actually go out and survey every individual person in the state and aggregate their responses? Was it based on a sample? Was it based on something else? So when you get data that are coarse, it's important to keep in mind how that data was aggregated. It's also important to take whatever conclusions you draw from that data set and limit them to the coarseness of your data set, right If you find there's a correlation in income between an income and voting.

Between states, then you say at the state level, the income is correlated with voting patterns in this way. That does not necessarily mean that individuals might also have the same correlation, right? You probably saw the ecological fallacy in data eight. You don't want to draw conclusions about individuals based on data about groups.
OK, so let's do an example. So think about that elections data set we've been working with for the last couple lectures. So what is each row represent?

And or in other words, what is the granularity of this data set? In this class, we'll use the word granularity to refer to like what is each row represent? We have to think and understand what is in the data. And sometimes this involves, you know, poking around and looking at it. Sometimes it involves going and looking up where you got the data from, reading up on it and understanding it better. But it's always important to be able to answer these kinds of questions at the beginning of your data exploration process. Otherwise you may end up coming up down with wrong conclusions downstream.
